topic: A person learns their childhood bully became a therapist
raw_output:
[story 1]

The sterile white of Dr. Albright’s office pressed in on me, amplifying the frantic rhythm of my own heartbeat. I sat on the edge of the leather chair, my hands clasped so tightly my knuckles were white islands in the gloom. Across the mahogany desk, he sat, impeccably composed, his gaze steady and unnervingly familiar. It was Mark. Mark, the architect of my early humiliations, the one whose sneer had carved deep grooves into my memory. Now, he wore a tailored suit and a calm, professional smile that felt like a physical assault.

“So,” Mark began, his voice a low, even baritone that seemed designed to soothe, “tell me about the pressure you feel when you try to connect with people.” I swallowed hard, the dryness in my throat making the sound catch. I kept glancing at the framed photograph on his desk—a younger Mark, all sharp angles and unchallenged confidence. The air thickened, heavy with unspoken history. I felt a sudden, overwhelming urge to bolt, to disappear into the beige walls.

“It’s… it’s like everything is too loud,” I managed, my voice a thin, reedy thing. I shifted my weight, suddenly aware of the cheap fabric of my sweater against my skin. “I can’t filter anything. It just floods in.” Mark leaned forward slightly, his expression shifting from professional neutrality to something sharper, something I recognized instantly—a knowing assessment. It was as if he were reading the topography of my anxiety without me having to utter a single confession.

[story 2]

The silence in the waiting room was suffocating. Every clock tick seemed to mock the relentless churning beneath my ribs. I kept running my tongue over my teeth, a nervous habit I hadn't shaken since I was twelve. When Mark finally called my name, I rose, my legs feeling unsteady, like they were made of water. Walking into the consultation room felt like stepping onto a stage where I was entirely unprepared.

He greeted me with a casual nod, the same easygoing gesture he used to deploy when he was about to deliver a cutting remark in the hallway. But his eyes, those familiar, assessing eyes, held a depth I hadn't anticipated. He didn't launch into probing questions immediately; instead, he simply gestured toward the comfortable armchair. “Take your time,” he advised, his tone suggesting that speed was an unnecessary performance.

I sank into the chair, the plush upholstery suddenly feeling too soft, too yielding. I focused intensely on the pattern of the rug, trying to anchor myself. The memory of his laughter, cruel and echoing, flickered behind my eyelids, sharp and unwelcome. I felt a tremor start in my hands, a slight vibration that ran up my forearms. When he finally spoke, his voice was gentle, almost hypnotic. “We start small. What is the smallest thing that causes you the most resistance right now?” I stared at him, my breath held captive in my chest, waiting for the inevitable judgment, the familiar sting of being scrutinized.

[story 3]

I kept catching my reflection in the polished surface of the consultation table. My face was pale, etched with a tension that felt permanent, like a strained muscle. I avoided looking directly at Mark, focusing instead on the subtle movement of his pen as he made notes. The atmosphere was charged, brittle, like thin ice about to crack under a heavy weight. I felt a profound sense of exposure, as if every insecurity I’d ever harbored was laid bare for his inspection.

“I don’t know where to begin,” I admitted, the words emerging in a rush, breathless and uneven. My voice cracked on the last word. I could feel the heat rising in my cheeks, a flush of shame that wanted to consume me. It was exhausting, this vulnerability, this forced excavation of buried things.

Mark set his pen down deliberately. The sound was crisp, definitive. He leaned back, crossing his arms, and for a moment, the familiar predatory gleam in his eyes returned, but it was tempered now, overlaid with a strange, almost weary empathy. “Then let’s start with the foundation,” he said, his tone shifting, becoming unexpectedly grounded. “Tell me what you think you *should* be feeling right now.” The directness of the challenge made me flinch internally, but beneath the shock, a tiny, fragile thread of curiosity began to pull at the surface of my composure.
